---
title: "When Do You Advance? Progression Rules After the First Prescription"
description: "Three progression logics govern when to advance a concussion aerobic prescription: weekly BCTT retest, CISG step grammar (24h floor on sport steps; symptom-gated on school steps), and session-titrated ramps. This installment maps each logic to its source trial, then asks whether the rules hold when return-to-learn and return-to-sport are running simultaneously."
date: "2026-06-24"
series: "Foundation Stack"
installment: pillar2-005
pillar: methods
---


::: {.callout-caution}
**Not medical advice. Educational use only. Data from published literature.**
:::

The prescription landed: walk at 65% age-predicted maximum heart rate, 20 minutes, five days a week. What changes it?

The Leddy/Buffalo group answers: bring the athlete back to the treadmill at the end of the week. Run a BCTT. If the threshold rose, raise the ceiling [PMID: 30715132](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30715132/). Micay et al. answers: add approximately 5% HRmax next session, on schedule [PMID: 30018795](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30018795/). The CISG consensus answers: for return-to-sport steps, a minimum of 24 hours per step; for return-to-learn steps, no equivalent time floor—only symptom threshold [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/). All three are in the adolescent SRC and PPCS literature. None has been compared head-to-head.

That is not a minor omission. The rule that governs advancement determines whether a student returns to full sport in three weeks or eight. It determines whether a partial school day is a gate or a side note. And in the week when return-to-learn and return-to-sport are both active—a student running PE drills and sitting through exam accommodations—it determines which track holds when symptom load climbs.

Three previous installments defined the prescription space: [*Six Protocols, One Condition*](../pillar2-001-six-protocols-comparison/index.html) mapped which protocols produce which intensity bands; [*No Treadmill in the Room*](../pillar2-002-no-bctt-prescription-paths/index.html) showed how to set a sub-symptom ceiling when treadmill access is unavailable; [*Twenty Minutes From Where?*](../pillar2-003-twenty-minutes-from-where/index.html) asked how much weekly volume fits a school schedule. [*Two Tracks, One Athlete*](../pillar2-004-school-vs-sport-staging/index.html) showed that the sport track and the school track are not running from the same document.

This installment asks the question all four deferred: **what is the published rule for changing a prescription that is already running, and does it hold when both tracks are active?**

**What this installment is—and is not.** This is a **methods** piece. It does not adjudicate which protocol produces better outcomes; no adolescent PPCS trial has randomized progression-rule type. It maps the three published logics to their source trials and clinical contexts, identifies where each logic was derived, and traces what happens when you apply each logic inside a dual-track return. Athletic trainers and school staff will find the [SOP table](#sop-dual-track) most actionable. Researchers and trainees will find the source comparisons in Sections 1 and 2 most useful.

#### 1. Three progression logics: rules and origins

The literature does not use the term "progression rule" consistently. Some papers describe operational trial visit schedules; others describe clinical conventions derived from those trials without formal derivation. The three logics below span that range.

One scope note before the map: Logic A and Logic C describe **when to change an aerobic prescription** (HR ceiling or session dose). Logic B describes **when to advance a return-to-sport step** in the CISG graduated strategy—a different object. In clinical practice, Logic B is routinely borrowed to time aerobic prescription changes because most RTS step 2 activity is the prescription; the two objects often collapse. This paper treats them as one category where they overlap in practice, while noting where the conflation matters.

##### 1.1 Logic A — Weekly BCTT retest (Leddy / Buffalo)

The Buffalo Concussion Treadmill Test (BCTT) was developed as both a diagnostic tool and a prescription anchor. In the foundational RCT [PMID: 30715132](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30715132/), Leddy et al. mandated weekly clinic returns:

> "Participants were seen every week to perform the BCTT and a new HR prescription was given until recovery."
> "A new target heart rate was determined by weekly clinic BCTT performance for as long as the participant remained symptomatic."

The logic is **test-gated**: the ceiling does not move until physiologic evidence of readiness is measured. In that trial's context—weekly visits, treadmill access, research protocol—this is operationally clean. The prescription stays fixed for seven days, the test reruns, and the ceiling adjusts based on where the threshold now sits.

The 2013 clinical guide from the same group [PMID: 24225521](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24225521/) softened the frequency requirement for outpatient settings: "The target HR is increased by 5 to 10 beats/min every 1 to 2 weeks, depending on how fast the patient is responding." That is still a **test-gated** or **time-gated** rule—no session-by-session titration—but at a practical cadence for clinics without weekly treadmill time.

Leddy 2021 [PMID: 34817719](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34817719/) described a stage-ladder fallback for settings between full BCTT access and no structure:

> "Patients who exercise for at least 20 min at 50% of HRmax without symptom exacerbation may advance to the next stage (55% of HRmax). If patients experience some worsening of symptoms during exercise at any stage (defined as a 1- or 2-point increase on a 0–10 pain scale compared with their pre-exercise value), they remain at the current stage until they can exercise without symptoms worsening. If patients experience significant worsening (3-point or more increase), the exercise intensity is reduced. Patients are encouraged to keep a diary to monitor their recovery and to be re-evaluated every 1–2 weeks. The goal is to exercise at 80% or more of HRmax without symptom exacerbation."

This is a **session-observed, test-lite** variant: it preserves the threshold logic but allows the previous session's tolerance to stand in for a formal BCTT. The re-evaluation cadence (every 1–2 weeks) aligns with the clinical guide interval rather than the weekly RCT visit schedule.

**Where this logic fits:** clinic-based PPCS programs with scheduled weekly or biweekly visits; post-acute concussion with treadmill access; any setting where the clinician will see the patient again before the prescription changes.

##### 1.2 Logic B — CISG step-advance grammar (Berlin, Amsterdam)

The CISG Berlin consensus [PMID: 28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/) paired a four-stage graduated return-to-school strategy alongside a six-step RTS protocol. The RTS protocol note reads: "There should be at least 24 hours (or longer) for each step of the progression. If any symptoms worsen during exercise, the athlete should go back to the previous step." The RTL table lists four activity stages (daily home tasks → homework/reading → part-time school → full return) with no per-stage time minimum stated; the only progression criterion is absence of symptom recurrence at the current stage.

Amsterdam 2022 [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/) made both tracks more explicit. The **RTS table 2 header** states: "each step typically takes a minimum of 24 hours." The **RTL table** (table 1) carries no such minimum; instead: "Progression through the strategy for students should be slowed when there is more than a mild and brief symptom exacerbation." In both tracks, the symptom threshold is identical:

> "*Mild and brief exacerbation of symptoms is defined as an increase of no more than 2 points on a 0–10 point scale (with 0 representing no symptoms and 10 the worst symptoms imaginable) for less than an hour when compared with the baseline value reported prior to cognitive activity." [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/)

The temporal grammar differs: **RTS has a floor** (minimum 24 hours per step); **RTL does not**. The RTL table specifies no minimum step-dwell; the only gate is symptom threshold. In practice this means RTL progression can be faster than RTS on a good run—but the Amsterdam text does not state a shortest step-interval, so "faster in principle" should not be read as "daily advance is intended." The same student on the sport track must wait at least 24 hours per RTS step regardless of session tolerance.

NICE NG232 (head injury guideline, 2023) uses similar symptom-guided step logic for return to activity without specifying a per-step time minimum—consistent with the CISG RTL grammar, not the RTS time floor.

**Where this logic fits:** community prescriptions, school-based programs, primary care, telehealth follow-up; any setting where weekly treadmill testing is impractical; the default grammar in CISG Berlin [PMID: 28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/), Amsterdam [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/), and NICE NG232.

##### 1.3 Logic C — Session-titrated prescriptions (Micay 2018; Pittsburgh SE)

Two published PPCS/SRC programs prescribe intensity changes on a **within-patient, within-episode calendar** rather than at weekly visits or per symptom stability.

**Micay 2018** [PMID: 30018795](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30018795/) ran a feasibility RCT in adolescent SRC with a structured 8-session ramp:

> "The first session lasted 10 minutes, at an intensity of 50% of the participant's age-predicted maximal heart rate, whereas the following 7 sessions lasted 20 minutes, with intensity increasing by 5% of the individual's age-predicted maximal heart rate per session."

The range is 50% to 70% age-predicted HRmax over 8 sessions. This is a **fixed ramp**: each completed session authorizes the next step, regardless of which day of the week it falls on or whether the BCTT threshold moved. The ramp is not clinician-adjusted per visit—it is written into the trial protocol and carried out by participants at home.

**Pittsburgh subsymptom exercise (Kurowski 2017)** [PMID: 27120294](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27120294/) used a different titration model: the clinic bike test identified the duration at which symptoms were first exacerbated, and the home dose was set to 80% of that duration. The dose adjusted at each of six clinic visits:

> "Participants were asked to complete their individually tailored cycling program 5–6 days per week at 80% of the duration that exacerbated symptoms during study visits. The cycling program was repeated at each of the following six visits and based on the results, the home exercise program was adjusted for each participant."

This is a **test-gated titration** like Logic A, but the progression metric is **duration** at a fixed effort ceiling, not absolute intensity. The ceiling itself does not step up by fixed percentages; instead the patient builds duration toward a target and intensity is reassessed when the duration target is met.

**Where these logics fit:** structured PT programs with defined session counts; home programs where the prescription is written for a set number of sessions and changes only at a clinic return; settings where repeated BCTT access is unavailable but session tracking is feasible.

##### Figure 1 — Three progression paths: hypothetical week-by-week intensity {#fig-three-logics}

```{r}
#| label: fig-three-logics
#| fig-width: 8.5
#| fig-height: 5
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(ggplot2)

fs_source_theme <- function() {
  candidates <- c(
    file.path(dirname(knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE)), "..", "_shared", "fs-ggplot-theme.R"),
    file.path(dirname(knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE)), "..", "..", "_shared", "fs-ggplot-theme.R"),
    "~/Documents/guanglab-foundation-stack/posts/_shared/fs-ggplot-theme.R"
  )
  path <- candidates[file.exists(candidates)][1]
  if (!is.na(path)) source(path, local = parent.frame())
}
fs_source_theme()

# Hypothetical 21-session (3-week) trajectory for a patient starting at 50% HRmax
# Logic A: weekly BCTT — ceiling advances by ~7% each week after clinic visit
# Logic B: CISG RTS step grammar (24h floor per RTS step) — schematic +2%/2 sessions (conservative RTS run)
# Logic C: Micay fixed ramp — +5% per completed session, cap at 70%

sessions <- 1:21

bctt <- c(
  rep(50, 5),   # week 1: 50% until BCTT retest
  rep(57, 5),   # week 2: 57% after first BCTT
  rep(65, 5),   # week 3: 65% after second BCTT
  rep(65, 6)
)[1:21]

stability <- c(
  50, 50, 52, 52, 54,
  54, 56, 56, 58, 58,
  60, 60, 63, 63, 66,
  66, 68, 68, 70, 70,
  72
)[1:21]

ramp <- pmin(50 + pmax(0, (seq_len(21) - 1)) * 1.25, 70)

df_prog <- data.frame(
  session = rep(sessions, 3),
  intensity = c(bctt, stability, ramp),
  logic = rep(c(
    "Logic A — Weekly BCTT retest\n(Leddy/Buffalo 2019)",
    "Logic B — CISG RTS step progression\n(24h floor/step; Amsterdam 2022)",
    "Logic C — Fixed ramp +5%/session\n(Micay 2018)"
  ), each = 21)
)

week_breaks <- c(5.5, 10.5, 15.5)

ggplot(df_prog, aes(x = session, y = intensity, color = logic, linetype = logic)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = week_breaks, linetype = "dotted", color = "#adb5bd", linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 2.8,  y = 74, label = "Wk 1", size = 3, color = FS_COL_MUTED) +
  annotate("text", x = 7.8,  y = 74, label = "Wk 2", size = 3, color = FS_COL_MUTED) +
  annotate("text", x = 12.8, y = 74, label = "Wk 3", size = 3, color = FS_COL_MUTED) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Logic A — Weekly BCTT retest\n(Leddy/Buffalo 2019)"     = FS_COL_CONSERVATIVE,
    "Logic B — CISG RTS step progression\n(24h floor/step; Amsterdam 2022)"  = FS_COL_PROGRESSIVE,
    "Logic C — Fixed ramp +5%/session\n(Micay 2018)"         = FS_COL_MODERATE
  )) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c(
    "Logic A — Weekly BCTT retest\n(Leddy/Buffalo 2019)"     = "solid",
    "Logic B — CISG RTS step progression\n(24h floor/step; Amsterdam 2022)"  = "dashed",
    "Logic C — Fixed ramp +5%/session\n(Micay 2018)"         = "dotdash"
  )) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 21, 2), minor_breaks = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(45, 78), breaks = seq(50, 75, 5),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title = "Three progression logics produce different three-week trajectories",
    subtitle = "Schematic only — hypothetical patient starting at 50% HRmax, uncomplicated course",
    x = "Session (numbered across weeks)",
    y = "Prescribed intensity (% age-predicted HRmax)",
    caption = paste0(
      "Logic A: ceiling held until weekly BCTT; schematic +7%/wk after retest [PMID: 30715132].\n",
      "Logic B: RTS step cadence with 24h floor (RTS table 2); schematic +2%/2 sessions. RTL has no equivalent floor—not plotted separately [PMID: 37316210].\n",
      "Logic C: +5% HRmax per session, cap 70%, 8-session Micay protocol [PMID: 30018795].\n",
      "Trajectories are illustrative. Real curves depend on symptom response and adherence."
    )
  ) +
  theme_fs(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.key.width = unit(1.6, "cm"))
```

**Figure 1.** All three logics start at 50% HRmax; trajectories diverge by session 3. Logic A holds the ceiling flat until a weekly treadmill test; the step jumps at weekly clinic visits. Logic B (dashed) models **RTS step cadence** with a 24h floor per step; the RTL track has no equivalent time floor and is not plotted separately. Logic C (dash-dot) advances mechanically by session count. A patient in the **same condition** following different logics would sit at materially different intensity ceilings by week 3—not because the science differs, but because the advancement grammar does.

#### 2. What drives the choice of progression logic?

No published trial has randomized progression logic type in adolescent PPCS. The assignment in practice follows setting constraints:

| Constraint | Logic that fits | Why |
|-----------|----------------|-----|
| Weekly treadmill access | A (BCTT retest) | Designed for this workflow; prescription change requires test |
| No treadmill; clinic seen biweekly | A fallback (5–10 bpm / 1–2 wk) or B | BCTT not available; symptom stability is observable without equipment |
| Structured PT, defined session count | C (Micay or Kurowski duration titration) | Ramp is pre-written; fewer clinical judgment calls mid-program |
| Home / school program only | B (CISG step grammar) | No treadmill; daily monitoring by parent or athlete; CISG / NICE NG232 |
| PPCS (>28 d symptoms) | A or C (caution with B alone) | Symptom volatility makes step-by-step symptom gates harder to read day-to-day; structured reassessment preferable |

The clinically dominant logic in North American published guidelines is **B (CISG step grammar)** because the Berlin and Amsterdam consensus documents [PMID: 28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/) [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/) specify symptom-gated step advance and it requires no equipment. The Buffalo/Leddy group's original trial logic is **A** but the published clinical guide already softened it to a 1–2 week interval without mandatory BCTT. Micay's **C** is the only one with a fixed-ramp RCT design in adolescents, but the sample was 18 participants and the protocol was explicitly labeled a feasibility study.

The takeaway is that the choice of progression logic is largely **determined by resource access and visit cadence**, not by evidence that one logic produces better outcomes. A practitioner who inherits a patient at 70% HRmax "because that's where the Micay ramp ended" and then holds that ceiling until a treadmill test is available is mixing Logic C for the ascent and Logic A for the hold—not an unreasonable clinical move, but one the literature has not studied as a hybrid.

One additional constraint from the volume literature: Howell 2021 [PMID: 33856860](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33856860/) found that ≥160 min/week was the observational threshold separating symptomatic from asymptomatic students with 81% accuracy on ROC analysis. That is a volume target, not a progression trigger—but it implies that advancing intensity may matter less than reaching consistent weekly minutes within any safe intensity band. The CISG RTS step-grammar track's potential for faster ascent (no weekly test required) may not produce better outcomes if the weekly session count is low; RTL's absence of a time floor adds further variability to combined daily load estimates.

#### 3. The dual-track layer: RTL and RTP do not share an advancement rule {#dual-track-advancement}

[*Two Tracks, One Athlete*](../pillar2-004-school-vs-sport-staging/index.html) showed that RTL and RTP run from different checklists and that the literature seldom tracks combined daily load. The progression-logic question now adds a second dimension: **does the RTL track use the same logic as the RTP track?**

The answer from the full text of both consensus documents: **same symptom threshold, but different temporal rules—and the difference is written into the table headers**.

Amsterdam 2022 uses the same ≤2pt / <1h exacerbation threshold for both RTL and RTP. But the **RTS table 2 is titled** "each step typically takes a minimum of 24 hours"; the **RTL table 1 carries no equivalent minimum**. Section 1.2 above quotes both table footnotes. This is not a coincidence: RTL progression is driven by daily cognitive tolerance, which can fluctuate hour by hour and cannot be cleanly bounded by a 24-hour window. A student in RTL step 2 doing three hours of exam review generates a symptom trajectory that has no clean start and end time the way a 20-minute walk does.

The operational consequence: the RTL table specifies no minimum step-dwell—the only gate is staying below the 2-point symptom threshold. The RTS track requires at least 24 hours per step even on perfect days. **These are not parallel rules; they are different clocks.**

No published protocol addresses this timing question directly. The macro rule is clear—Amsterdam: "student-athletes should complete full RTL before unrestricted RTS" [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/); Berlin: "children and adolescents should not return to sport until they have successfully returned to school" [PMID: 28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/)—but those are **track-completion gates**, not same-day symptom-budget rules.

##### 3.1 Which track gates the other when both are active?

In the early weeks of return—RTL steps 2–3 and RTP steps 1–2 simultaneously—both tracks are live and both generate daily symptom load. Amsterdam 2022 states: "while the RTL and RTS strategies can occur in parallel, student-athletes should complete full RTL before unrestricted RTS" [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/). That is a track-completion gate, not a session-level budget rule. It does not say what to do on a day where a 20-minute walk was symptom-free but an afternoon of reduced-load homework caused a 3/10 spike for 90 minutes.

The SOP section below proposes a practical default. Two publications provide indirect support:

**Wingerson 2024** [PMID: 38109187](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38109187/) found that concurrent school and exercise loads are associated with longer symptom duration in some subgroups, without identifying a safe combined budget. The signal is operational difficulty, not a specific rule.

**DeMatteo 2019** [PMID: 31396150](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31396150/) ran RTL (school) and RTA (activity) tracks simultaneously in stages 1–3 for 139 youth patients and found that RTA stage 6 (full contact) was appropriately deferred until full school reintegration. The stages overlapped safely in lower steps; the hold was at the high-contact end. That matches Amsterdam's explicit gate.

The default in the absence of a published rule: **if either track generates >2/10 escalation for >1 hour on a given day, hold advancement on both tracks the next day**. This is conservative and may slow the sport track unnecessarily for students who are genuinely ahead on RTL. If a school-side spike is clearly attributable to a discrete event (e.g., exam day) and has fully resolved, clinicians may document the cause and reassess rather than applying a mechanical hold. It is preferable to the alternative—advancing sport independently of school load—which chart-review literature identifies as the most common premature return pattern [PMID: 35292472](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35292472/) [PMID: 24925965](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24925965/).

##### 3.2 Yang 2024: cognitive activity is not the hazard

One common clinical assumption in dual-track management is that cognitive load should be aggressively minimized to protect the aerobic recovery budget. Yang et al. 2024 [PMID: 38346776](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38346776/) found the reverse in an acute SRC cohort: more moderate-to-vigorous cognitive activity was associated with **faster** symptom resolution (adjusted HR 1.22 per additional 10 minutes of moderate-to-high cognitive activity daily). Delayed return to school was associated with an **8% reduction** in resolution rate.

This is an observational acute SRC study, not an adolescent PPCS trial, and Yang et al. note that relatively little has been reported on optimal cognitive activity intensity post-concussion. The finding does not override the ≤2/10 symptom cap from Amsterdam. But it does reframe the dual-track concern: the documented hazard is not cognitive load itself—it is **uncoordinated tracking**, where aerobic advancement proceeds without documentation of school-side load and the combined symptom picture is never assembled.

A student who is advancing well on RTP step 2 aerobic sessions while sitting in RTL step 2 with mounting exam accommodations is not necessarily in a dangerous cognitive-load situation. They may be in a **documentation gap** where neither the sport trainer nor the school counselor knows what the other's checklist says. That is the gap Yang 2024 indirectly supports closing—not the suppression of cognitive activity.

#### 4. The combined-load progression gap {#combined-load-gap}

A review of the published concussion literature found **no protocol that specifies a combined RTL + RTP load budget for progression decisions**. The macro coordination rules (RTL before unrestricted RTP; symptom-gated step advance) are established in the CISG consensus [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/) [PMID: 28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/). The session-level question—if school and aerobic exercise both provoke symptoms on the same day, which track's advancement rule governs?—is not addressed in any trial or consensus document reviewed for this installment.

This gap sits at the intersection of the three logics:

- If using **Logic A** (weekly BCTT), the treadmill test is unaffected by what happened at school that week—the prescription changes only if the BCTT threshold moved. School symptoms on Tuesday do not delay Friday's test. This is operationally clean but clinically incomplete: a student who is struggling through RTL step 3 with daily headaches is plausibly not a good candidate for a BCTT intensity bump, regardless of what the treadmill shows.

- If using **Logic B** (CISG step grammar), the same-day cognitive load is invisible unless the clinician specifically asks. The student reports "the walk was fine," which is true. The clinician advances the RTP step. Nobody asked about the three-hour afternoon of screen-based schoolwork.

- If using **Logic C** (fixed ramp), session completion drives advancement automatically. The ramp schedule does not have a school-symptom input.

What fills this gap is **clinical judgment and documentation practice**—specifically, a dual-column format that records school-side and sport-side status on the same day. [*Two Tracks, One Athlete*](../pillar2-004-school-vs-sport-staging/index.html) showed that most SRC discharge plans carry only one track's checklist; DeMatteo 2019 found that concurrent RTL and RTP stages were tracked separately in practice, with no single document recording combined daily load [PMID: 31396150](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31396150/). Until a trial reports combined daily load with preregistered advancement rules, the field's silence on this question should be acknowledged explicitly in the record, not papered over with a generic "advancing as tolerated" note.

#### 5. Limitations

1. **No head-to-head trial of progression logics in adolescent PPCS.** Logic A (BCTT) is derived from a trial that was not designed to compare progression rules; Logic C (Micay) was a feasibility RCT with *n* = 18. Logic B (CISG step grammar) is consensus-derived, not trial-derived.

2. **RTL vs RTS temporal asymmetry is under-reported in secondary literature.** Full text of Amsterdam 2022 [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/) confirms that the "minimum 24 hours per step" language appears in the **RTS table 2 header only**—not in the RTL table. Berlin 2017 [PMID: 28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/) similarly states "at least 24 hours for each step" in the RTS protocol note, with no equivalent RTL minimum. This asymmetry—RTS time-gated, RTL symptom-gated only—is not consistently flagged in clinical summaries and reviews of the consensus statements.

3. **Yang 2024 is acute SRC, not PPCS.** The cognitive-activity finding (aHR 1.22) applies to post-acute return to learn in acutely concussed adolescents [PMID: 38346776](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38346776/). Generalizability to PPCS—where symptom profiles differ—is not established.

4. **Figure 1 is schematic.** The three progression trajectories are illustrative based on published rules, not empirical data from tracked patients. Week-3 intensities would differ substantially based on symptom response, adherence, and setting.

5. **Micay protocol coverage.** The Micay 2018 feasibility RCT enrolled patients within 2 weeks of SRC, not PPCS. The ramp logic is relevant but the sample and timeline may not generalize to prolonged symptom patients.

6. **No adolescent-PPCS RCT with dual-track combined outcome.** All progression logics reviewed here were tested with RTP outcomes as primary; RTL was tracked secondarily or not at all.

#### 6. SOP: dual-track progression decision checklist {#sop-dual-track}

*Synthesizes three progression logics and CISG dual-track coordination rules for educational use only [PMID: 30715132](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30715132/) [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/) [PMID: 30018795](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30018795/) [PMID: 27120294](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27120294/) [PMID: 38346776](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38346776/).*

##### Step 0 — Assign a progression logic at first prescription

| Setting | Default logic | Required at each reassessment |
|---------|--------------|-------------------------------|
| Clinic with weekly treadmill | Logic A (BCTT retest) | New BCTT threshold → new ceiling |
| Structured PT, fixed program | Logic C (session ramp or duration titration) | Session completion + symptom log |
| Community / school / telehealth | Logic B (CISG step grammar; RTS: ≥24h floor) | Daily symptom log; ≤2/10 gate |

Documenting which logic was assigned prevents the "advancing as tolerated" ambiguity from accumulating across handoffs.

##### Step 1 — Advance decision for each track

| Track | Advance when | Hold when |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| **RTP aerobic (step 1→2, 2A→2B)** | Previous session complete; symptoms ≤2/10 at peak, resolved <1h; **RTL gate not blocking** | >2/10 for >1h on any activity that session day; RTL step incomplete for age-appropriate contact level |
| **RTL (step 1→2→3→4)** | Prior step tolerated without >mild exacerbation; academic support stable | Symptom escalation on same day as aerobic session, regardless of whether walk was symptom-free |

##### Step 2 — Same-day combined-load rule (proposed default)

This rule has no published source. It is a clinician-judgment bridge for the gap identified in Section 4:

> **If either track generates >2/10 symptom escalation for >1 hour on a given day, defer advancement on both tracks the next morning.** Reassess after one more symptom-stable day on the lower step.

Document the reason for the hold. If RTL repeatedly blocks RTP advancement, flag for clinical review rather than silently holding—the limiting factor may need attention (academic accommodation inadequacy, exam timing, or clinical reassessment of PPCS trajectory).

##### Step 3 — High-contact gate

Per Amsterdam 2022 youth guidance [PMID: 37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/):

> **No RTP steps 4–6** (team drills, contact, unrestricted play) until **RTL step 4** is reached (full school day without accommodations, no concussion-related academic support).

This is a hard gate in the published consensus. It cannot be waived by logic A (BCTT test result) or logic C (session count completed).

##### Dual-track status line (copy to any communication)

> **RTL: Step __ / 4** (e.g., partial day, __ accommodations active) · **RTP: Step __ / 6** · Progression logic: [BCTT weekly / CISG step grammar / session ramp] · Combined-load rule applied: [yes / no] · Next reassessment: __

The HR calculator from [*No Treadmill in the Room*](/tools/hr-calculator/) encodes intensity bands for RTP step 2. Apply within the step ceiling; do not use as permission to advance across step boundaries independently of RTL status.

::: {.callout-important}
**Educational use only.** Progression rules summarized here are derived from published trials and consensus statements. They do not replace individualized medical assessment, local return-to-play law, or school board policy.
:::

#### What this installment added

Three progression logics are operating in the adolescent SRC and PPCS literature simultaneously: weekly BCTT retest (test-gated, clinic-dependent), CISG step grammar with a 24h floor for RTS steps (symptom-gated on both tracks; no equivalent time floor on RTL), and session-titrated ramps (calendar-gated, program-dependent). None has been compared head-to-head in adolescent PPCS. The choice in practice is determined primarily by setting constraints—treadmill access, visit cadence, supervision—not by evidence that one logic produces better outcomes.

When both return-to-learn and return-to-sport are active, the advancement rule used on one track does not automatically apply to the other. The macro coordination rules (full RTL before unrestricted RTP) are established. The daily combined-load question—what happens to advancement when school and sport both generate symptoms on the same day—is a gap in the published literature that clinical judgment currently fills.

If [*Six Protocols, One Condition*](../pillar2-001-six-protocols-comparison/index.html) asked which protocol sets the first ceiling, [*No Treadmill in the Room*](../pillar2-002-no-bctt-prescription-paths/index.html) asked how to set that ceiling without a treadmill, [*Twenty Minutes From Where?*](../pillar2-003-twenty-minutes-from-where/index.html) asked how much volume fits a school week, and [*Two Tracks, One Athlete*](../pillar2-004-school-vs-sport-staging/index.html) asked what coordinates the two checklists—this installment asks what changes the ceiling once it is running. The answer is: it depends on which logic was assigned, and the assigned logic should be documented.

The [RSS feed](../../index.xml) on the hub will notify readers when the next installment publishes. The next installment works through a different unresolved problem in the same literature.

---

**Series:** [Foundation Stack](/index.html) · **pillar2-005** · **Pillar:** methods (progression rules; dual-track advancement)
